title: Asthma, Constriction, and Voice You Couldn't Use
dek: *When your chest tightens and breath shortens, your body may be doing more than reacting to allergens. It may be holding the shape of every sentence you couldn't say, every boundary you didn't voice, every moment you made yourself smaller to keep the peace in a room that never felt safe enough for your full presence.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Asthma often tightens around unexpressed anger, creating a physical cage for words you never spoke. Your airways mirror the constriction in your throat when you swallow protest, apology, or truth. Learning to breathe fully again means learning to speak what you've been holding back.

What if the tightness in your chest isn't just about pollen or dust, but about all the times you held your tongue when you wanted to scream? I'm not talking about asthma as metaphor. I'm talking about the embodied reality that our respiratory system doesn't distinguish between a physical threat and an emotional one. Your airways constrict when you encounter an allergen, yes. But they also tighten when you're in a room where speaking up feels dangerous, when you're swallowing words that need to come out, when you're holding your breath waiting for someone else's anger to pass. In my own practice, I've watched this pattern repeat: the inhaler becomes necessary not just during pollen season, but during family gatherings. Not just when the air quality drops, but when the emotional pressure rises. Your body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk taught us, and sometimes it keeps that score in the very tissues that govern your ability to breathe and speak. ## The Architecture of Holding Back Your diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscle that sits below your lungs. When you inhale, it flattens and descends, creating space. When you exhale, it rises back up, pushing air out. This is mechanical, yes. But it's also emotional. Watch a child who's about to cry but has been told not to. Watch how their breathing changes. They take shallow sips of air, high in the chest. The diaphragm barely moves. They're literally holding their breath, holding their tears, holding their voice. Now imagine doing that for years. Imagine doing it so consistently that your body forgets there was ever another way. Somatic psychologist Pat Ogden has spent decades mapping how trauma lives in the body's posture and breath. She points out that we don't just remember difficult experiences cognitively. We hold them in chronic muscle tension, in breathing patterns, in the way we brace against the world. When you couldn't speak as a child, when it wasn't safe to express anger or pain or need, your body found ways to contain all that energy. The throat tightens. The chest becomes rigid. The breath stays shallow. Asthma can have a genetic component. It can be triggered by genuine allergens and irritants. And it can also be the body's way of maintaining a protective pattern that once kept you safe. If raising your voice brought punishment, if expressing anger meant abandonment, if speaking your truth shattered the fragile peace, then of course your airways learned to close. Of course your body found a way to keep those dangerous words locked inside. ## The Breath You're Not Taking Sit with this for a moment. Right now, as you're reading, notice your breath. Don't change it yet. Just notice. Is it high in your chest or deep in your belly? Is it smooth or jagged? Are you breathing through your nose or your mouth? Are you holding anywhere? Most people discover they've been breathing shallowly without realizing it. We walk around taking in just enough air to survive, but not enough to thrive. Not enough to speak powerfully. Not enough to feel fully. There's a reason public speaking terrifies so many people. It's not really about the audience. It's about the breath required to project your voice, to take up space with sound, to let yourself be heard. For someone who learned early that being heard was dangerous, that's a primal threat. The body responds accordingly. Heart rate spikes. Airways tighten. You literally can't catch your breath. Thich Nhat Hanh taught that conscious breathing is a return to the present moment, a way of coming home to yourself. But what he also understood, and wrote about extensively, is that many of us are afraid of that homecoming. We're afraid of what we'll find when we stop running, stop distracting, stop holding our breath. We're afraid of the anger we've been sitting on. The grief we've been postponing. The truth we've been avoiding. Your asthma inhaler opens your airways chemically. That's vital. That saves lives. And there's another kind of opening available, one that happens when you start to exhale the words you've been holding, when you start to let your voice carry the weight it was meant to carry. ## What Anger Has to Do With It Karla McLaren, in her new work on emotional intelligence, repositions anger as a boundary-setting emotion. It's not a destructive force to be managed and controlled. It's information. It arises when your boundaries have been crossed, when something isn't right, when you need to take action to protect yourself or restore balance. But what happens when you're not allowed to set boundaries? What happens when your anger is met with punishment, dismissal, or rage that dwarfs your own? You learn to swallow it. You learn to smile when you want to shout. You learn to say "it's fine" when nothing is fine. That swallowed anger doesn't disappear. It becomes inflammation. It becomes muscle tension. It becomes shallow breathing and tight airways. Across the articles we've published on this site, this pattern emerges again and again: the body will express what the voice cannot. The APA's research summary on anger management confirms what many of us know intuitively: suppressed anger contributes to a range of physical symptoms, including respiratory issues. The anger doesn't go away when you swallow it. It just goes inward. I've sat with people who've had asthma their entire lives, who've managed it with medication and avoidance of triggers, who've done everything right medically. And then something shifts. They leave an abusive relationship. They finally confront a parent. They quit a job where they were silenced daily. And their asthma improves. Not always dramatically. Not always completely. But enough to notice. Enough to wonder. This isn't about blaming yourself for your asthma. It's about recognizing that your body is wise, that it's been protecting you the best way it knows how, and that there might be another layer of healing available if you're ready to explore it. ## The Practice: Speaking Into Space Here's something you can try right now, something that takes less than five minutes and requires nothing but privacy and willingness. Find a room where you can be alone. Stand up. Place one hand on your belly and one on your chest. Take three deep breaths, feeling your belly expand with each inhale. Let your chest hand stay relatively still. This is diaphragmatic breathing, the kind that gives you power and steadiness. Now think of something you wish you'd said but didn't. It doesn't have to be dramatic. Maybe it's "I need help" or "That hurt me" or "I'm angry about this." Something true that you swallowed. Speak it out loud. Not loudly, necessarily. But clearly. Let yourself hear your own voice saying the thing. Notice what happens in your chest, your throat, your breath. Do you want to stop? Do you feel relief? Does your breath catch? Try it three times, each time a little more fully. Let your voice carry the weight of the truth. Let your diaphragm support the sound. Let yourself take up space with your words. This isn't about confronting anyone. This is about teaching your body that it's safe to speak, that your voice can carry truth without catastrophe, that breathing and speaking can happen together. In our experience, this simple practice can shift something fundamental. Not all at once. But enough to start. ## The Body Remembers What the Mind Forgets Your nervous system has been shaped by every interaction you've ever had. When you were three and you cried and someone held you, your body learned that distress brings comfort. When you were seven and you got angry and someone hit you, your body learned that anger brings danger. When you were twelve and you spoke up and everyone went silent, your body learned that your voice disrupts peace. These aren't conscious memories. They're somatic imprints, written into your muscle memory and your breathing patterns and your stress responses. You don't remember the specific moment your airways learned to tighten. You just notice that they do. The respiratory system is intimately connected to the vagus nerve, that wandering pathway that regulates your stress response. When your vagus nerve detects threat, real or perceived, it can trigger bronchoconstriction. Your airways narrow. Your breath becomes shallow. Your body is trying to protect you, using the same strategies that kept you safe when you were small and powerless. But you're not small anymore. You're not powerless anymore. And your body can learn new patterns if you teach it gently, consistently, with compassion for how hard it's been working all these years to keep you safe. Traditional Chinese Medicine has long recognized the connection between lung health and grief, between the metal element and the capacity to let go. [Plum Flower Gui Pi Wan Economy (1000 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003A6AXPI?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one formula practitioners sometimes use to support both digestive and respiratory systems when there's a pattern of overthinking and holding on. Western herbalism offers adaptogens like [Gaia Herbs Ashwagandha Root (180 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BRT9PQFC?tag=spankyspinola-20) to help the nervous system find more flexibility, more capacity to shift between states without getting stuck in chronic stress. These aren't replacements for your inhaler. They're supports for the deeper work of teaching your body that it can relax, that it can breathe fully, that it can trust you to protect yourself with your voice instead of with constriction. ## When Your Voice Becomes Available Again There's a moment, if you do this work, when you notice your voice sounds different. Richer, maybe. More resonant. Coming from deeper in your body. It's not dramatic. You might not even notice it yourself until someone else comments. This is what happens when you start breathing from your diaphragm instead of your upper chest. When you stop holding your breath between sentences. When you let yourself speak without apologizing for taking up space. Your voice is an extension of your breath. When your breath is shallow and restricted, your voice is too. It's thin, uncertain, easy to talk over. When your breath is full and supported, your voice carries weight. People listen differently. You listen differently to yourself. Over the years I've seen people reclaim their voices in stages. First, they notice they're not breathing. Then they practice breathing more fully. Then they start speaking small truths in safe spaces. Then bigger truths. Then necessary truths to people who need to hear them. And somewhere in that process, the inhaler gets used less. The tight chest happens less often. The body starts to trust that speaking is survivable. This isn't linear. You don't heal your relationship with your voice and suddenly never have asthma symptoms again. But you might notice that your triggers shift. That the emotional component becomes more obvious. That you have more choice in how you respond. If you're curious about the intersection of emotional work and physical healing, you might find kindred spirits at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on the spiritual dimensions of this same territory. Here, we're staying with the body, with the breath, with the practical reality of living in a system that learned to protect you by keeping you quiet. ## Unlearning the Constriction Your body learned to constrict for good reasons. Maybe the adults around you couldn't handle your big feelings. Maybe your anger was too much for them, so they shamed it into silence. Maybe speaking up meant being seen, and being seen meant being hurt. Maybe you just learned that small and quiet was safer than large and loud. Whatever the reason, it made sense at the time. Your body's protective strategies aren't mistakes. They're adaptations. They kept you alive. They got you here. And now you get to choose whether they're still serving you. Now you get to gently, gradually teach your body that there are other ways to be safe. That you can set boundaries with your words instead of with constricted airways. That you can breathe fully even when you're uncomfortable. That your voice is allowed to take up space. [Honor Your Anger - Beverly Engel](https://www.amazon.com/dp/0806540788?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers practical frameworks for this unlearning, for recognizing anger as legitimate and learning to express it without either suppressing it or exploding. It's not about becoming an angry person. It's about becoming an honest person who can feel and express the full range of human emotion without your body having to step in and do the expressing for you. There's a tenderness required in this work. You're not forcing your body to change. You're inviting it. You're creating conditions where it can relax, where it can try something new, where it can discover that speaking and breathing can happen together without catastrophe. ## Questions People Actually Ask ### Can emotional work really help with asthma, or is this just wishful thinking? Emotional work isn't a replacement for medical treatment, and it won't cure asthma that has purely physiological causes. But research increasingly shows that stress, suppressed emotion, and nervous system dysregulation can trigger or worsen respiratory symptoms. Working with the emotional and somatic dimensions doesn't mean your asthma isn't real. It means you're addressing all the factors that contribute to it, not just the mechanical ones. Keep your inhaler. Keep your medical care. And add the layer of breath work, voice work, and emotional processing. They work together, not instead of each other. ### How do I know if my asthma has an emotional component? Notice the patterns. Does it get worse in certain emotional situations, around certain people, during certain kinds of conversations? Do you notice yourself holding your breath when you're anxious or angry? Do you feel tightness in your chest when you want to say something but don't? These aren't definitive proof, but they're clues. You might also notice that your breathing improves when you're in environments where you feel safe to speak freely, where you don't have to monitor and control your expression. Your body will tell you if you're willing to listen. ### What if I try to speak and nothing comes out, or I start crying instead? That's information. That's your body showing you how much has been held back, how long you've been silent. Crying is a form of exhale, a release of pressure. Let it happen. The words will come when your system feels safe enough to let them. You might need to practice speaking in writing first, or speaking to a therapist, or speaking to yourself in a mirror. You're teaching your body that expression is safe. That takes time, especially if you've spent years learning the opposite. Be patient with yourself. You can also explore [more articles in this journal](/articles) for additional practices and perspectives on this kind of embodied healing work. ## Opening the Cage There's a moment in the fairy tale where the princess finds her voice and the spell breaks. The tower crumbles. The kingdom wakes. Everything changes in an instant. Real life isn't like that. Real life is you noticing you held your breath through an entire difficult conversation. Real life is you speaking one true sentence and then needing to lie down because it took everything you had. Real life is your chest tight for days after you finally said no to someone who's used to hearing yes. But real life is also this: one day you notice you took a full breath without thinking about it. One day you say something true and your airways stay open. One day you realize you haven't needed your rescue inhaler in weeks. One day your voice sounds like it's coming from your whole body, not just your throat. The cage opens slowly. Each breath is a key. Each word you speak that you would have swallowed before is another bar removed. You're not healing from asthma. You're healing into your full capacity to breathe, to speak, to take up the space you were always meant to occupy. Your body has been waiting for this. Your voice has been waiting. And the air you've been rationing? There's enough for everything you need to say. There always was. You just needed to learn it was safe to take it in. If you're looking for herbal allies to support your respiratory and nervous systems as you do this work, [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) offers starting points for exploration. And if you want to know more about the philosophy behind this approach to healing, [the about page](/about) lays out our foundations. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on asthma, constriction, and voice you couldn't use, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
